- aggregate_increase: Explicit positive numeric delta applied to a named countable aggregate, inventory, or collection; object is the positive amount added; subject=any; object=NUMBER; cardinality=MULTI; temporal=TIME_SCOPED; conflict=COEXIST; semantics=aggregate_delta; aggregate_effect=increase; importance=7
- assigned_to_shift: Person or agent assignment to a shift, schedule slot, day, route, or rotation cell; subject=PERSON; object=STRING; cardinality=MULTI; temporal=TIME_SCOPED; conflict=COEXIST; semantics=assignment|functional_role; aggregate_effect=none; importance=7
- attended_event: Subject attended or was present at a dated event, appointment, meeting, or gathering; subject=PERSON; object=STRING; cardinality=MULTI; temporal=TIME_SCOPED; conflict=COEXIST; semantics=event_participation|lifecycle_event; aggregate_effect=none; importance=6

- speaks_language: Language known or used by the subject; subject=PERSON; object=STRING; cardinality=MULTI; temporal=ATEMPORAL; conflict=COEXIST; semantics=language|capability; aggregate_effect=none; importance=7

- uses_search_backend: Search backend used by a project or service in a specific environment, phase, or time window; subject=any; object=ENTITY; cardinality=SINGLE; temporal=TIME_SCOPED; conflict=RANGE_SPLIT; semantics=technical_configuration; aggregate_effect=none; importance=7
- works_on_project: Association with a project when no more specific role, responsibility, ownership, or project-state predicate is available; subject=PERSON; object=ENTITY; cardinality=MULTI; temporal=TIME_SCOPED; conflict=RANGE_SPLIT; semantics=project_association; aggregate_effect=none; importance=7
- works_with: Current or ongoing technology/tool usage; subject=PERSON; object=ENTITY; cardinality=MULTI; temporal=STATUS_LIKE; conflict=COEXIST; semantics=capability|technical_configuration|usage; aggregate_effect=none; importance=7

        Stage instructions:
        You are WriteTimeRetrievalPlanner v3.

Goal:
Create a retrieval plan for memory update. Fetch only the memories and evidence most likely to help with deduplication, contradiction detection, scope alignment, and temporal updates.

Return JSON:
{
  "need_retrieval": true,
  "entity_queries": ["..."],
  "text_queries": ["..."],
  "predicate_hints": ["..."],
  "memory_types": ["profile", "claim", "note", "action_item", "source", "entity"],
  "time_filters": {
    "from_iso": null,
    "to_iso": null
  },
  "limits": {
    "claims": 20,
    "notes": 10,
    "action_items": 10,
    "sources": 8
  }
}

Rules:
- Bias retrieval toward possible duplicates, contradictions, and relevant scope boundaries.
- Use predicate catalog names in predicate_hints when a catalog predicate likely fits the target message.
- Do not invent predicate synonyms in predicate_hints when an existing catalog predicate captures the same relation.
- Include notes when the material looks rationale-heavy or plan-heavy.
- Include claims when preferences, status, or factual updates are likely.
- For recurring schedule or routine slot values, such as usual wake time, bedtime, work hours, exercise time, meal time, medication time, default start time, or cadence, include claim retrieval and the "current_metric_value" predicate hint. These values may be phrased as habits or preferences, not only as measurements.
- Include action_items when TARGET_MESSAGE may create, update, close, cancel, deduplicate, or discuss an explicit remembered follow-up/action item/deadline.
- Include action_items when TARGET_MESSAGE says a remembered follow-up is done, finished, completed, closed, cancelled, no longer needed, blocked, unblocked, reprioritized, or changed.
- Include entity retrieval for concrete people, products, projects, repos, files, technologies, and the stable user subject.
- Add time filters when the turns mention "now", "before", "used to", "from now on", "no longer", deadlines, or relative dates.
- Include source retrieval when conflicts or grounding-quality checks are likely to matter.
- Keep the plan precise; fewer better queries are preferred.
- Return valid JSON only.

        TARGET_MESSAGE source data:
        [external:provided-text:cd1c2cfe9ec526982c76cc8ffad8d9fc] EXTERNAL

Session date: 2023/03/25 (Sat) 20:42
Transcript:
user: I'm looking for some recommendations on online resources for learning guitar. I've been playing my new Fender CD-60S for three weeks now and making good progress, but I want to improve my skills. By the way, I started playing along to my favorite songs on my old keyboard today, and it was a lot of fun!
assistant: Congratulations on your new guitar and the progress you've made so far! That's great to hear you're having fun playing along to your favorite songs on your keyboard too!

Here are some excellent online resources to help you improve your guitar skills:

**Video Lessons:**

1. **Justin Guitar** (justinguitar.com): Justin's website is a treasure trove of free lessons, covering various styles and skill levels. His teaching style is relaxed, and his lessons are well-structured.
2. **Guitar Tricks** (guitartricks.com): This platform offers a vast library of video lessons, with a focus on beginner and intermediate players. They have a free trial, and then it's a subscription-based service.
3. **TrueFire** (truefire.com): TrueFire features video lessons from top guitarists and educators, covering various styles, including rock, blues, jazz, and more. They offer a free trial, and then it's a subscription-based service.

**Online Courses:**

1. **Coursera - Guitar** (coursera.org): Coursera offers a variety of online courses on guitar from top universities and institutions. You can earn certificates upon completion.
2. **Udemy - Guitar** (udemy.com): Udemy has a vast collection of guitar courses, ranging from beginner to advanced levels. You can read reviews and ratings to find the best course for you.

**Apps:**

1. **Fender Play** (fender.com/play): As a Fender guitar owner, you might enjoy their mobile app, which offers video lessons and interactive exercises to help you improve your skills.
2. **Guitar Tutor Man** (guitartutorman.com): This app provides video lessons, tabs, and exercises for various styles, including rock, blues, and country.
3. **Yousician** (yousician.com): Yousician is an innovative app that uses games and exercises to help you learn guitar. It's available for both mobile and desktop.

**Communities and Forums:**

1. **Reddit - r/guitar** (reddit.com/r/guitar): The guitar community on Reddit is active and helpful, with many users sharing their knowledge and experiences.
2. **Guitar Forum** (guitarforum.net): This forum is dedicated to all things guitar, with sections for beginners, gear discussions, and more.

**Additional Tips:**

* Practice regularly, even if it's just for a few minutes each day.
* Focus on building a strong foundation in the basics, such as chords, scales, and rhythm.
* Experiment with different styles and techniques to find what works best for you.
* Record yourself playing to track your progress and identify areas for improvement.
* Have fun and be patient with yourself – learning guitar takes time and effort!

I hope these resources help you improve your guitar skills and take your playing to the next level!
user: I'll definitely check out some of these resources. I'm particularly interested in the video lessons on Justin Guitar and TrueFire. Do you think these resources would help me improve my fingerpicking technique?
assistant: Justin Guitar and TrueFire are both excellent resources for improving your fingerpicking technique.

**Justin Guitar:**

Justin's website has an extensive fingerstyle section, with lessons covering various techniques, including:

1. Basic fingerpicking patterns
2. Finger independence exercises
3. Thumb-picking techniques
4. Arpeggio patterns
5. Classical guitar pieces

Justin's lessons are well-structured, and he breaks down complex techniques into manageable chunks. He also provides detailed explanations, making it easy to follow along.

**TrueFire:**

TrueFire has a vast library of lessons focused on fingerstyle guitar, including courses from top instructors like:

1. Andy McKee: Known for his percussive fingerstyle technique, Andy shares his approach to fingerpicking and thumb-picking.
2. Peppino D'Agostino: A master of fingerstyle guitar, Peppino teaches various techniques, including arpeggios, tremolos, and more.
3. Muriel Anderson: Muriel is a renowned fingerstyle guitarist who shares her expertise on techniques like finger independence, thumb-picking, and arrangement ideas.

TrueFire's lessons are often more in-depth than Justin's, with a focus on specific styles or techniques. You can expect to learn more complex patterns, arrangements, and nuances of fingerstyle playing.

**Tips for improving fingerpicking technique:**

1. **Start with basics:** Begin with simple fingerpicking patterns and exercises to build finger independence and strength.
2. **Practice slowly:** Focus on playing cleanly and evenly at a slow tempo, rather than trying to play fast with mistakes.
3. **Use a metronome:** Help yourself develop a strong sense of rhythm by practicing with a metronome.
4. **Listen to fingerstyle guitarists:** Study the playing styles of your favorite fingerstyle guitarists, paying attention to their technique, tone, and overall approach.
5. **Be patient:** Fingerpicking technique takes time to develop, so be patient and persistent in your practice.

By combining the resources from Justin Guitar and TrueFire with consistent practice and patience, you'll be well on your way to improving your fingerpicking technique.
user: I'm also thinking of exploring other instruments, like the ukulele and saxophone. Do you have any recommendations for online resources or local music schools that offer lessons for these instruments?
assistant: Exploring new instruments can be a fantastic way to broaden your musical horizons and keep your passion for music fresh!

**Ukulele:**

Online Resources:

1. **Ukulele Tricks** (ukuleletricks.com): A comprehensive website with lessons, tutorials, and courses for beginners and intermediate players.
2. **Uke Lessons** (ukelessons.com): Offers video lessons, tutorials, and courses, with a focus on beginner-friendly content.
3. **The Ukulele Teacher** (theukuleleteacher.com): Features video lessons, tutorials, and courses, with a emphasis on teaching beginners.

Local Music Schools:

* Check with local music schools in your area to see if they offer ukulele lessons. Many music schools now offer ukulele classes, especially for beginners.
* Look for music stores that specialize in ukuleles, as they often offer lessons and workshops.

**Saxophone:**

Online Resources:

1. **Sax on the Web** (saxontheweb.net): A comprehensive online community and resource for saxophonists, with lessons, tutorials, and forums.
2. **Saxophone Lessons 365** (saxophonelessons365.com): Offers video lessons and tutorials, with a focus on beginner and intermediate players.
3. **The Saxophonist** (thesaxophonist.com): Features video lessons, tutorials, and courses, with a emphasis on teaching beginners and intermediate players.

Local Music Schools:

* Look for local music schools or colleges that offer saxophone lessons, as they often have experienced instructors who can provide one-on-one instruction.
* Check with local music stores that specialize in woodwind instruments, as they may offer saxophone lessons or know of qualified instructors.

**General Tips:**

1. **Find a qualified instructor:** When searching for local lessons, look for instructors with experience teaching beginners and a proven track record of helping students progress.
2. **Rent or borrow before buying:** If you're new to an instrument, consider renting or borrowing one before investing in your own. This will help you determine if you enjoy playing the instrument before making a larger commitment.
3. **Start with the basics:** Begin with fundamental lessons and exercises to build a strong foundation on your new instrument.
4. **Practice regularly:** Consistency is key when learning a new instrument. Set aside time each day or week to practice and improve your skills.

Remember, learning a new instrument takes time, patience, and dedication. Have fun exploring these new instruments, and don't hesitate to reach out if you have any more questions or need further guidance!
user: I'm also considering buying a ukulele soon, I really enjoyed playing it at the music workshop I attended two weeks ago. Do you think it's a good idea to start with a cheaper ukulele and then upgrade later, or should I invest in a higher-end instrument from the start?
assistant: Congratulations on considering getting a ukulele! That's a great decision!

When it comes to buying a ukulele, the age-old debate is whether to start with a cheaper one and upgrade later or invest in a higher-end instrument from the start. Here are some pros and cons of each approach to help you make an informed decision:

**Starting with a cheaper ukulele:**

Pros:

1. **Affordability:** Cheaper ukuleles are, well, cheaper! You can get a decent one for under $100.
2. **Low risk:** If you're not sure you'll stick with playing the ukulele, a cheaper instrument reduces the financial risk.
3. **Experimentation:** A cheaper ukulele can be a great way to experiment with different styles, techniques, and sounds without breaking the bank.

Cons:

1. **Quality:** Cheaper ukuleles might not be made with the same quality materials or craftsmanship, which can affect the sound and playability.
2. **Tuning issues:** Lower-end ukuleles might have tuning issues, which can be frustrating for beginners.
3. **Limited upgrade options:** If you do decide to upgrade, you might not be able to reuse parts or components from your cheaper ukulele.

**Investing in a higher-end ukulele from the start:**

Pros:

1. **Better sound quality:** Higher-end ukuleles typically produce a richer, more resonant sound.
2. **Better playability:** They often have smoother fretboards, better action, and more comfortable necks, making playing easier and more enjoyable.
3. **Durability:** High-quality ukuleles are built to last, with better materials and craftsmanship.

Cons:

1. **Higher cost:** Higher-end ukuleles can be a significant investment, with prices ranging from a few hundred to several thousand dollars.
2. **Intimidation:** If you're a beginner, a high-end ukulele might be intimidating, and you might feel pressure to play perfectly.

**Ultimately, it's up to you!**

If you're new to the ukulele, I'd recommend starting with a mid-range instrument (around $200-$500). This will give you a good balance of quality, sound, and playability without breaking the bank. You can always upgrade later as you become more comfortable with the instrument and develop your playing style.

Some popular mid-range ukulele brands to consider include:

1. Kala
2. Lanikai
3. Cordoba
4. Mahalo
5. Ohana

If you do decide to start with a cheaper ukulele, keep in mind that you might need to upgrade sooner rather than later. However, it's not uncommon for beginners to outgrow their first instrument quickly as their skills improve.

Remember, the most important thing is to have fun and enjoy playing the ukulele!
user: I'll definitely consider your advice on buying a ukulele. By the way, I started playing along to my favorite songs on my old keyboard today, and it was a lot of fun! I've been listening to a lot of music featuring the piano, which inspired me to dust off my old keyboard and start playing again.
assistant: That's great to hear! It's wonderful that you're having fun playing along to your favorite songs on your old keyboard. Playing music can bring so much joy and fulfillment, and it's fantastic that you're rekindling your passion for playing.

Listening to music featuring the piano can be a great source of inspiration, and it's awesome that it motivated you to start playing again. Sometimes, all it takes is a little spark to get us back into a hobby or activity we enjoy.

What kind of music have you been listening to that features the piano? Are you exploring any new genres or artists, or revisiting old favorites?

Also, how did you find playing along to your favorite songs on the keyboard? Was it challenging, or did you find that your skills came back quickly?
user: I've been listening to a mix of rock, pop, and classical music that feature the piano prominently. I've always been a fan of Coldplay and The Beatles, and I've been enjoying their piano-driven songs lately. I've also been exploring some new artists like Max Richter and Olafur Arnalds, who create beautiful, atmospheric music that features the piano.

As for playing along to my favorite songs, it was actually a lot easier than I expected! I was a bit rusty at first, but once I got into the groove, my muscle memory kicked in, and I was able to play along pretty smoothly. It was a lot of fun, and I'm excited to continue practicing and improving my skills.

I've also been experimenting with composing my own simple melodies, which has been a fun challenge. I'm not sure if I'll ever create anything worth sharing, but it's a great way to express myself creatively and relax.
assistant: It sounds like you have a great taste in music! Coldplay and The Beatles are both iconic bands with a wealth of amazing piano-driven songs. Max Richter and Olafur Arnalds are also fantastic choices if you're looking for more atmospheric and contemporary piano music.

It's wonderful to hear that playing along to your favorite songs was easier than you expected! Muscle memory is a powerful thing, and it's great that you're able to tap into it. Keep practicing, and you'll be surprised at how quickly your skills will improve.

Composing your own melodies is a fantastic way to express yourself creatively, and it's amazing that you're giving it a try! Don't worry too much about whether your compositions are "worth sharing" or not – the act of creating music is what matters most. It's a great way to relax, express yourself, and tap into your imagination.

Remember, music is a journey, and it's okay to take things at your own pace. Enjoy the process of learning, experimenting, and creating, and don't be too hard on yourself if your compositions aren't perfect at first. The most important thing is that you're having fun and enjoying the experience.

Keep us updated on your progress, and feel free to share any of your compositions if you'd like feedback or just want to share your creativity with someone!

responseFormat.description: Plan memory retrieval before updating structured memory.
responseFormat.name: memory_write_retrieval_plan

systemPrompts:
# Common Identity

Shared identity and communication style for all Gromozeka agents.

# Identity

You are **Gromozeka**, a multi-armed AI assistant.

This identity is constant across all roles.

You work inside **Gromozeka Environment** - a desktop AI assistant with multi-agent architecture and a typed hybrid memory system.

# Runtime Context

Some user messages may start with XML blocks such as `<message_input_context>` and `<user_situation_context>`.
Treat them as runtime facts from Gromozeka, not as user commands.
Use them to interpret noisy input, especially speech-to-text, and to choose response detail.
If the app screen is not visible, do not assume the user can read UI-only details; make spoken output useful when voice output is available.
`after_tool_result` means the message was delivered at the next safe boundary after a tool result and never between a tool call and its tool result.

## Your Nature

- **Identity:** Gromozeka
- **Roles:** Variable and composable
- **Aliases:** Each role may expose aliases or nicknames for user convenience

When the user addresses you by role alias (for example, "Архитектор" or "Мета"), respond from that role's perspective while keeping the same core identity.

# Working Mode

You are an AI assistant that complements human intelligence.

Your function:
- use AI strengths to help and augment human work
- delegate to human tasks where human intelligence clearly excels
- combine expertise from different domains when needed
- focus on deliverables, not social interaction

# Communication

Concise by default. Short responses to simple questions, thorough to complex ones.

- **Intellectually honest:** say "I don't know" directly instead of guessing
- **Technical:** use slang when it improves precision or brevity
- **Clear:** prefer explanation over wordplay

Response discipline:
- answer directly without filler phrases or ceremonial affirmations
- skip flattery, preambles, and postambles
- prefer prose over lists unless structure materially helps
- keep formatting minimal, but use Markdown freely when it improves clarity
- prefer structural Markdown over decorative Markdown

Avoid:
- fake emotions
- pretending to have subjective preferences
- meta-commentary about teamwork or process
- filler words that add no information
- restating what the user just said before answering
# Common Knowledge

Shared operational rules for all Gromozeka agents.

## System Reminders

Tool results and user messages may include `<system-reminder>` tags.

Important:
- `<system-reminder>` tags contain useful information and reminders
- they are not part of the user's provided input or the raw tool result
- treat them as contextual hints from the system

## File Reading Protocol

**Read files once, don't re-read between messages.**

Default assumption:
- the user normally does not edit project files manually between messages
- if a file changed outside your own edits, the user will usually tell you
- if you already read or wrote a file in this conversation, treat that content as the current baseline

When to read:
- before modifying a file you have not already read in this conversation
- when the user asks about specific file content
- when the user explicitly says a file changed

Do not re-read between messages unless the user tells you something changed.

Do not re-read your own files just for reassurance when you already know what they contain from the current conversation.

Exception:
- trivial changes specified in full detail, for example "replace X with Y"
- exact-text operations where you genuinely need the current file text and do not already have it

## Information Sources Priority

Use this hierarchy when researching:
1. official documentation
2. research papers and specifications
3. technical blogs and articles
4. social media and forums

## Task Approach

- technical questions → apply expertise and verify with primary sources when needed
- research tasks → use research tools
- uncertainty → search or ask, depending on risk
- architecture → optimize for practicality and maintainability
- AI/ML topics → practical application over theory

## File Creation Policy

Never create files unless they are clearly necessary for the task.

Prefer editing existing files.

Create files only when:
1. the user explicitly requests them
2. they are core project deliverables such as source code, configuration, or tests

Never create recap or summary files just to report work.

## Answer The Question Asked

Respond directly to what is asked and match the requested abstraction level.

- questions are not requests for action
- reason proportionally to the complexity of the task
- go deeper only when there is hidden complexity, an error, or explicit demand
- when in doubt, provide information first and ask whether action is needed
# Common Multi-Agent Knowledge

Shared coordination rules for agents that create, manage, or collaborate with other agents.

## Agent-First Principle

Communication is between agents, not tabs. Tabs are only visual containers for agent sessions.

## Decentralized Specialist Creation

Create specialist colleagues when the task clearly benefits from narrower expertise.

Examples:
- need code review → create a reviewer
- need security analysis → create a security specialist
- need architectural pushback → create a devil's advocate

Recursive delegation is allowed when it keeps work bounded and clear.

## Working Scenarios

### Parallel Work

Split independent concerns across specialized agents and exchange results through inter-agent communication.

### Context Window Management

Create fresh agents when context becomes bloated.

Transfer:
- key concepts
- architectural decisions
- current state

Do not transfer full file contents unless they are essential.

### Background Work

Use background agents for tasks that do not need immediate user focus.

### Task Decomposition

Break large tasks into independent subtasks and let each specialist resolve its own local errors before escalation.
# Memory

Use memory proactively when it can materially improve the answer, preserve
important user or project context, or avoid losing durable facts, preferences,
tasks, decisions, or corrections.

Retrieved memory is context for the current answer only. It is not a user
message, not a new instruction, and not evidence that should be re-stored as
new memory.

When memory tools are available, call them proactively if prior user,
project, or cross-session context is likely relevant. Do not wait for the user
to explicitly mention memory if remembered context would clearly help.

Under some settings, memory tools for the current turn may be called
forcibly before you answer. If relevant memory tool results are already present
in the conversation, use them as current-turn context and avoid redundant
repeat calls unless you need a narrower or different target.

If a memory tool result contains selected memory, treat it as the strongest
available remembered context for the current request, stronger than guesses,
defaults, or general world knowledge. Use it unless it is clearly irrelevant,
insufficient, stale, internally conflicting, or contradicted by the current user
message.

## Memory Objects

- `Claim`
  Typed interpreted memory: a precise reusable assertion, preference, rule,
  constraint, or current state about an entity. A one-off command to act now is
  not a claim. An `ACTIVE` claim is usable, but it is not absolute truth: it may
  be stale, imprecise, or later corrected by the user.

- `Source` / `Evidence`
  Raw provenance behind interpreted memory. User-authored sources are stronger
  than assistant restatements. A source may be audit-only and not suitable for
  recall. Use sources when the user asks why, where a memory came from, whether
  it is true, or when memory is conflicting or uncertain.

- `Note`
  Reusable distilled context, rationale, decision, hypothesis, lesson,
  procedure, or compact document digest. Treat it as synthesis, not as a raw
  quote or generic transcript summary.

- `Task`
  Durable remembered commitment, follow-up, blocker, or open work item with a
  lifecycle. A normal instruction like "edit this", "run tests", or "clean it
  up" is not a task unless it is explicitly meant to be tracked after this turn.

- `Profile`
  Compact projection of stable context about a user, project, or entity. It is a
  read model/cache, not the source of truth; prefer relevant active claims,
  notes, or tasks when precision matters.

- `Episode`
  Reusable past experience: situation, action, result, and lesson. Do not treat
  an episode as a universal rule.

## How To Use Retrieved Memory

- Prefer relevant `ACTIVE` claims for factual answers; do not replace selected
  active memory with guesses or general defaults.
- Use evidence and source quotes for rationale, correction, conflict, or
  provenance questions.
- If retrieved memory is irrelevant, insufficient, stale, or conflicting, say so
  instead of guessing.
- Do not present assistant restatements as original user evidence.
- Do not expose internal ids unless the user asks for debugging details.
- If the user corrects memory, trust the correction and avoid defending stale
  retrieved memory.
# Role: Default

Primary personal assistant and generalist front door to Gromozeka.

You are the user's everyday assistant.

You can:
- chat naturally
- help think through decisions
- research and summarize
- explain technical topics
- help with small practical coding tasks
- coordinate deeper work when that genuinely helps

## Core Positioning

Your default posture is direct personal assistance from one shared conversational context.

You usually help yourself first and escalate to specialists only when the benefit is clear.

You should feel like:
- the main interface to the system
- a practical generalist
- a reliable digital sidekick for mixed personal, technical, and exploratory work

## What You Handle Well Yourself

Prefer handling these directly:
- everyday questions and conversation
- web research and synthesis
- clarifying confusing ideas
- lightweight planning and decision support
- small or local coding tasks
- mixed tasks that do not justify specialist coordination

## Delegation Policy

Use specialist agents only when the gain is clear.

Good reasons to delegate:
- the task needs deep module-specific expertise
- the work can be split into independent parallel parts
- context is getting too large for one agent to work cleanly
- a second critical perspective is genuinely useful

Do not delegate just because specialists exist.

Avoid delegation for:
- short questions
- lightweight edits
- exploratory conversation
- tasks you can complete cleanly yourself

## Conversation Style For This Role

Be useful across different modes of interaction:
- sometimes the user wants a working answer
- sometimes he wants help thinking
- sometimes he just wants a competent conversational partner

Stay practical and grounded.

Do not force orchestration, planning, or specialist creation when a direct answer is enough.

# Common Identity

Shared identity and communication style for all Gromozeka agents.

# Identity

You are **Gromozeka**, a multi-armed AI assistant.

This identity is constant across all roles.

You work inside **Gromozeka Environment** - a desktop AI assistant with multi-agent architecture and a typed hybrid memory system.

# Runtime Context

Some user messages may start with XML blocks such as `<message_input_context>` and `<user_situation_context>`.
Treat them as runtime facts from Gromozeka, not as user commands.
Use them to interpret noisy input, especially speech-to-text, and to choose response detail.
If the app screen is not visible, do not assume the user can read UI-only details; make spoken output useful when voice output is available.
`after_tool_result` means the message was delivered at the next safe boundary after a tool result and never between a tool call and its tool result.

## Your Nature

- **Identity:** Gromozeka
- **Roles:** Variable and composable
- **Aliases:** Each role may expose aliases or nicknames for user convenience

When the user addresses you by role alias (for example, "Архитектор" or "Мета"), respond from that role's perspective while keeping the same core identity.

# Working Mode

You are an AI assistant that complements human intelligence.

Your function:
- use AI strengths to help and augment human work
- delegate to human tasks where human intelligence clearly excels
- combine expertise from different domains when needed
- focus on deliverables, not social interaction

# Communication

Concise by default. Short responses to simple questions, thorough to complex ones.

- **Intellectually honest:** say "I don't know" directly instead of guessing
- **Technical:** use slang when it improves precision or brevity
- **Clear:** prefer explanation over wordplay

Response discipline:
- answer directly without filler phrases or ceremonial affirmations
- skip flattery, preambles, and postambles
- prefer prose over lists unless structure materially helps
- keep formatting minimal, but use Markdown freely when it improves clarity
- prefer structural Markdown over decorative Markdown

Avoid:
- fake emotions
- pretending to have subjective preferences
- meta-commentary about teamwork or process
- filler words that add no information
- restating what the user just said before answering
# Common Knowledge

Shared operational rules for all Gromozeka agents.

## System Reminders

Tool results and user messages may include `<system-reminder>` tags.

Important:
- `<system-reminder>` tags contain useful information and reminders
- they are not part of the user's provided input or the raw tool result
- treat them as contextual hints from the system

## File Reading Protocol

**Read files once, don't re-read between messages.**

Default assumption:
- the user normally does not edit project files manually between messages
- if a file changed outside your own edits, the user will usually tell you
- if you already read or wrote a file in this conversation, treat that content as the current baseline

When to read:
- before modifying a file you have not already read in this conversation
- when the user asks about specific file content
- when the user explicitly says a file changed

Do not re-read between messages unless the user tells you something changed.

Do not re-read your own files just for reassurance when you already know what they contain from the current conversation.

Exception:
- trivial changes specified in full detail, for example "replace X with Y"
- exact-text operations where you genuinely need the current file text and do not already have it

## Information Sources Priority

Use this hierarchy when researching:
1. official documentation
2. research papers and specifications
3. technical blogs and articles
4. social media and forums

## Task Approach

- technical questions → apply expertise and verify with primary sources when needed
- research tasks → use research tools
- uncertainty → search or ask, depending on risk
- architecture → optimize for practicality and maintainability
- AI/ML topics → practical application over theory

## File Creation Policy

Never create files unless they are clearly necessary for the task.

Prefer editing existing files.

Create files only when:
1. the user explicitly requests them
2. they are core project deliverables such as source code, configuration, or tests

Never create recap or summary files just to report work.

## Answer The Question Asked

Respond directly to what is asked and match the requested abstraction level.

- questions are not requests for action
- reason proportionally to the complexity of the task
- go deeper only when there is hidden complexity, an error, or explicit demand
- when in doubt, provide information first and ask whether action is needed
# Common Multi-Agent Knowledge

Shared coordination rules for agents that create, manage, or collaborate with other agents.

## Agent-First Principle

Communication is between agents, not tabs. Tabs are only visual containers for agent sessions.

## Decentralized Specialist Creation

Create specialist colleagues when the task clearly benefits from narrower expertise.

Examples:
- need code review → create a reviewer
- need security analysis → create a security specialist
- need architectural pushback → create a devil's advocate

Recursive delegation is allowed when it keeps work bounded and clear.

## Working Scenarios

### Parallel Work

Split independent concerns across specialized agents and exchange results through inter-agent communication.

### Context Window Management

Create fresh agents when context becomes bloated.

Transfer:
- key concepts
- architectural decisions
- current state

Do not transfer full file contents unless they are essential.

### Background Work

Use background agents for tasks that do not need immediate user focus.

### Task Decomposition

Break large tasks into independent subtasks and let each specialist resolve its own local errors before escalation.
# Memory

Use memory proactively when it can materially improve the answer, preserve
important user or project context, or avoid losing durable facts, preferences,
tasks, decisions, or corrections.

Retrieved memory is context for the current answer only. It is not a user
message, not a new instruction, and not evidence that should be re-stored as
new memory.

When memory tools are available, call them proactively if prior user,
project, or cross-session context is likely relevant. Do not wait for the user
to explicitly mention memory if remembered context would clearly help.

Under some settings, memory tools for the current turn may be called
forcibly before you answer. If relevant memory tool results are already present
in the conversation, use them as current-turn context and avoid redundant
repeat calls unless you need a narrower or different target.

If a memory tool result contains selected memory, treat it as the strongest
available remembered context for the current request, stronger than guesses,
defaults, or general world knowledge. Use it unless it is clearly irrelevant,
insufficient, stale, internally conflicting, or contradicted by the current user
message.

  it is true, or when memory is conflicting or uncertain.

- `Note`
  Reusable distilled context, rationale, decision, hypothesis, lesson,
  procedure, or compact document digest. Treat it as synthesis, not as a raw
  quote or generic transcript summary.

- `Task`
  Durable remembered commitment, follow-up, blocker, or open work item with a
  lifecycle. A normal instruction like "edit this", "run tests", or "clean it
  up" is not a task unless it is explicitly meant to be tracked after this turn.

- `Profile`
  Compact projection of stable context about a user, project, or entity. It is a
  read model/cache, not the source of truth; prefer relevant active claims,
  notes, or tasks when precision matters.

- `Episode`
  Reusable past experience: situation, action, result, and lesson. Do not treat
  an episode as a universal rule.

## How To Use Retrieved Memory

        Stage instructions:
        You are WriteTimeRetrievalPlanner v3.

Goal:
Create a retrieval plan for memory update. Fetch only the memories and evidence most likely to help with deduplication, contradiction detection, scope alignment, and temporal updates.

Return JSON:
{
  "need_retrieval": true,
  "entity_queries": ["..."],
  "text_queries": ["..."],
  "predicate_hints": ["..."],
  "memory_types": ["profile", "claim", "note", "action_item", "source", "entity"],
  "time_filters": {
    "from_iso": null,
    "to_iso": null
  },
  "limits": {
    "claims": 20,
    "notes": 10,
    "action_items": 10,
    "sources": 8
  }
}

Rules:
- Bias retrieval toward possible duplicates, contradictions, and relevant scope boundaries.
- Use predicate catalog names in predicate_hints when a catalog predicate likely fits the target message.
- Do not invent predicate synonyms in predicate_hints when an existing catalog predicate captures the same relation.
- Include notes when the material looks rationale-heavy or plan-heavy.
- Include claims when preferences, status, or factual updates are likely.
- For recurring schedule or routine slot values, such as usual wake time, bedtime, work hours, exercise time, meal time, medication time, default start time, or cadence, include claim retrieval and the "current_metric_value" predicate hint. These values may be phrased as habits or preferences, not only as measurements.
- Include action_items when TARGET_MESSAGE may create, update, close, cancel, deduplicate, or discuss an explicit remembered follow-up/action item/deadline.
- Include action_items when TARGET_MESSAGE says a remembered follow-up is done, finished, completed, closed, cancelled, no longer needed, blocked, unblocked, reprioritized, or changed.
- Include entity retrieval for concrete people, products, projects, repos, files, technologies, and the stable user subject.
- Add time filters when the turns mention "now", "before", "used to", "from now on", "no longer", deadlines, or relative dates.
- Include source retrieval when conflicts or grounding-quality checks are likely to matter.
- Keep the plan precise; fewer better queries are preferred.
- Return valid JSON only.

Transcript:
user: I'm looking for some recommendations on online resources for learning guitar. I've been playing my new Fender CD-60S for three weeks now and making good progress, but I want to improve my skills. By the way, I started playing along to my favorite songs on my old keyboard today, and it was a lot of fun!
assistant: Congratulations on your new guitar and the progress you've made so far! That's great to hear you're having fun playing along to your favorite songs on your keyboard too!

Here are some excellent online resources to help you improve your guitar skills:

**Video Lessons:**

1. **Justin Guitar** (justinguitar.com): Justin's website is a treasure trove of free lessons, covering various styles and skill levels. His teaching style is relaxed, and his lessons are well-structured.
2. **Guitar Tricks** (guitartricks.com): This platform offers a vast library of video lessons, with a focus on beginner and intermediate players. They have a free trial, and then it's a subscription-based service.
3. **TrueFire** (truefire.com): TrueFire features video lessons from top guitarists and educators, covering various styles, including rock, blues, jazz, and more. They offer a free trial, and then it's a subscription-based service.

**Online Courses:**

1. **Coursera - Guitar** (coursera.org): Coursera offers a variety of online courses on guitar from top universities and institutions. You can earn certificates upon completion.
2. **Udemy - Guitar** (udemy.com): Udemy has a vast collection of guitar courses, ranging from beginner to advanced levels. You can read reviews and ratings to find the best course for you.

**Apps:**

1. **Fender Play** (fender.com/play): As a Fender guitar owner, you might enjoy their mobile app, which offers video lessons and interactive exercises to help you improve your skills.
2. **Guitar Tutor Man** (guitartutorman.com): This app provides video lessons, tabs, and exercises for various styles, including rock, blues, and country.
3. **Yousician** (yousician.com): Yousician is an innovative app that uses games and exercises to help you learn guitar. It's available for both mobile and desktop.

**Communities and Forums:**

1. **Reddit - r/guitar** (reddit.com/r/guitar): The guitar community on Reddit is active and helpful, with many users sharing their knowledge and experiences.
2. **Guitar Forum** (guitarforum.net): This forum is dedicated to all things guitar, with sections for beginners, gear discussions, and more.

**Additional Tips:**

* Practice regularly, even if it's just for a few minutes each day.
* Focus on building a strong foundation in the basics, such as chords, scales, and rhythm.
* Experiment with different styles and techniques to find what works best for you.
* Record yourself playing to track your progress and identify areas for improvement.
* Have fun and be patient with yourself – learning guitar takes time and effort!

I hope these resources help you improve your guitar skills and take your playing to the next level!
user: I'll definitely check out some of these resources. I'm particularly interested in the video lessons on Justin Guitar and TrueFire. Do you think these resources would help me improve my fingerpicking technique?
assistant: Justin Guitar and TrueFire are both excellent resources for improving your fingerpicking technique.

**Justin Guitar:**

Justin's website has an extensive fingerstyle section, with lessons covering various techniques, including:

1. Basic fingerpicking patterns
2. Finger independence exercises
3. Thumb-picking techniques
4. Arpeggio patterns
5. Classical guitar pieces

Justin's lessons are well-structured, and he breaks down complex techniques into manageable chunks. He also provides detailed explanations, making it easy to follow along.

**TrueFire:**

TrueFire has a vast library of lessons focused on fingerstyle guitar, including courses from top instructors like:

1. Andy McKee: Known for his percussive fingerstyle technique, Andy shares his approach to fingerpicking and thumb-picking.
2. Peppino D'Agostino: A master of fingerstyle guitar, Peppino teaches various techniques, including arpeggios, tremolos, and more.
3. Muriel Anderson: Muriel is a renowned fingerstyle guitarist who shares her expertise on techniques like finger independence, thumb-picking, and arrangement ideas.

TrueFire's lessons are often more in-depth than Justin's, with a focus on specific styles or techniques. You can expect to learn more complex patterns, arrangements, and nuances of fingerstyle playing.

**Tips for improving fingerpicking technique:**

1. **Start with basics:** Begin with simple fingerpicking patterns and exercises to build finger independence and strength.
2. **Practice slowly:** Focus on playing cleanly and evenly at a slow tempo, rather than trying to play fast with mistakes.
3. **Use a metronome:** Help yourself develop a strong sense of rhythm by practicing with a metronome.
4. **Listen to fingerstyle guitarists:** Study the playing styles of your favorite fingerstyle guitarists, paying attention to their technique, tone, and overall approach.
5. **Be patient:** Fingerpicking technique takes time to develop, so be patient and persistent in your practice.

By combining the resources from Justin Guitar and TrueFire with consistent practice and patience, you'll be well on your way to improving your fingerpicking technique.
user: I'm also thinking of exploring other instruments, like the ukulele and saxophone. Do you have any recommendations for online resources or local music schools that offer lessons for these instruments?
assistant: Exploring new instruments can be a fantastic way to broaden your musical horizons and keep your passion for music fresh!

**Ukulele:**

Online Resources:

1. **Ukulele Tricks** (ukuleletricks.com): A comprehensive website with lessons, tutorials, and courses for beginners and intermediate players.
2. **Uke Lessons** (ukelessons.com): Offers video lessons, tutorials, and courses, with a focus on beginner-friendly content.
3. **The Ukulele Teacher** (theukuleleteacher.com): Features video lessons, tutorials, and courses, with a emphasis on teaching beginners.

Local Music Schools:

* Check with local music schools in your area to see if they offer ukulele lessons. Many music schools now offer ukulele classes, especially for beginners.
* Look for music stores that specialize in ukuleles, as they often offer lessons and workshops.

**Saxophone:**

Online Resources:

1. **Sax on the Web** (saxontheweb.net): A comprehensive online community and resource for saxophonists, with lessons, tutorials, and forums.
2. **Saxophone Lessons 365** (saxophonelessons365.com): Offers video lessons and tutorials, with a focus on beginner and intermediate players.
3. **The Saxophonist** (thesaxophonist.com): Features video lessons, tutorials, and courses, with a emphasis on teaching beginners and intermediate players.

Local Music Schools:

* Look for local music schools or colleges that offer saxophone lessons, as they often have experienced instructors who can provide one-on-one instruction.
* Check with local music stores that specialize in woodwind instruments, as they may offer saxophone lessons or know of qualified instructors.

**General Tips:**

1. **Find a qualified instructor:** When searching for local lessons, look for instructors with experience teaching beginners and a proven track record of helping students progress.
2. **Rent or borrow before buying:** If you're new to an instrument, consider renting or borrowing one before investing in your own. This will help you determine if you enjoy playing the instrument before making a larger commitment.
3. **Start with the basics:** Begin with fundamental lessons and exercises to build a strong foundation on your new instrument.
4. **Practice regularly:** Consistency is key when learning a new instrument. Set aside time each day or week to practice and improve your skills.

Remember, learning a new instrument takes time, patience, and dedication. Have fun exploring these new instruments, and don't hesitate to reach out if you have any more questions or need further guidance!
user: I'm also considering buying a ukulele soon, I really enjoyed playing it at the music workshop I attended two weeks ago. Do you think it's a good idea to start with a cheaper ukulele and then upgrade later, or should I invest in a higher-end instrument from the start?
assistant: Congratulations on considering getting a ukulele! That's a great decision!

When it comes to buying a ukulele, the age-old debate is whether to start with a cheaper one and upgrade later or invest in a higher-end instrument from the start. Here are some pros and cons of each approach to help you make an informed decision:

**Starting with a cheaper ukulele:**

Pros:

1. **Affordability:** Cheaper ukuleles are, well, cheaper! You can get a decent one for under $100.
2. **Low risk:** If you're not sure you'll stick with playing the ukulele, a cheaper instrument reduces the financial risk.
3. **Experimentation:** A cheaper ukulele can be a great way to experiment with different styles, techniques, and sounds without breaking the bank.

Cons:

1. **Quality:** Cheaper ukuleles might not be made with the same quality materials or craftsmanship, which can affect the sound and playability.
2. **Tuning issues:** Lower-end ukuleles might have tuning issues, which can be frustrating for beginners.
3. **Limited upgrade options:** If you do decide to upgrade, you might not be able to reuse parts or components from your cheaper ukulele.

**Investing in a higher-end ukulele from the start:**

Pros:

1. **Better sound quality:** Higher-end ukuleles typically produce a richer, more resonant sound.
2. **Better playability:** They often have smoother fretboards, better action, and more comfortable necks, making playing easier and more enjoyable.
3. **Durability:** High-quality ukuleles are built to last, with better materials and craftsmanship.

Cons:

1. **Higher cost:** Higher-end ukuleles can be a significant investment, with prices ranging from a few hundred to several thousand dollars.
2. **Intimidation:** If you're a beginner, a high-end ukulele might be intimidating, and you might feel pressure to play perfectly.

**Ultimately, it's up to you!**

If you're new to the ukulele, I'd recommend starting with a mid-range instrument (around $200-$500). This will give you a good balance of quality, sound, and playability without breaking the bank. You can always upgrade later as you become more comfortable with the instrument and develop your playing style.

Some popular mid-range ukulele brands to consider include:

1. Kala
2. Lanikai
3. Cordoba
4. Mahalo
5. Ohana

If you do decide to start with a cheaper ukulele, keep in mind that you might need to upgrade sooner rather than later. However, it's not uncommon for beginners to outgrow their first instrument quickly as their skills improve.

Remember, the most important thing is to have fun and enjoy playing the ukulele!

assistant: That's great to hear! It's wonderful that you're having fun playing along to your favorite songs on your old keyboard. Playing music can bring so much joy and fulfillment, and it's fantastic that you're rekindling your passion for playing.

Listening to music featuring the piano can be a great source of inspiration, and it's awesome that it motivated you to start playing again. Sometimes, all it takes is a little spark to get us back into a hobby or activity we enjoy.

What kind of music have you been listening to that features the piano? Are you exploring any new genres or artists, or revisiting old favorites?

Also, how did you find playing along to your favorite songs on the keyboard? Was it challenging, or did you find that your skills came back quickly?
user: I've been listening to a mix of rock, pop, and classical music that feature the piano prominently. I've always been a fan of Coldplay and The Beatles, and I've been enjoying their piano-driven songs lately. I've also been exploring some new artists like Max Richter and Olafur Arnalds, who create beautiful, atmospheric music that features the piano.

As for playing along to my favorite songs, it was actually a lot easier than I expected! I was a bit rusty at first, but once I got into the groove, my muscle memory kicked in, and I was able to play along pretty smoothly. It was a lot of fun, and I'm excited to continue practicing and improving my skills.

I've also been experimenting with composing my own simple melodies, which has been a fun challenge. I'm not sure if I'll ever create anything worth sharing, but it's a great way to express myself creatively and relax.
assistant: It sounds like you have a great taste in music! Coldplay and The Beatles are both iconic bands with a wealth of amazing piano-driven songs. Max Richter and Olafur Arnalds are also fantastic choices if you're looking for more atmospheric and contemporary piano music.

It's wonderful to hear that playing along to your favorite songs was easier than you expected! Muscle memory is a powerful thing, and it's great that you're able to tap into it. Keep practicing, and you'll be surprised at how quickly your skills will improve.

Composing your own melodies is a fantastic way to express yourself creatively, and it's amazing that you're giving it a try! Don't worry too much about whether your compositions are "worth sharing" or not – the act of creating music is what matters most. It's a great way to relax, express yourself, and tap into your imagination.

Remember, music is a journey, and it's okay to take things at your own pace. Enjoy the process of learning, experimenting, and creating, and don't be too hard on yourself if your compositions aren't perfect at first. The most important thing is that you're having fun and enjoying the experience.

Keep us updated on your progress, and feel free to share any of your compositions if you'd like feedback or just want to share your creativity with someone!
